titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Margaret Ruxton, September 16, 1819
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Wake Forest University, University of Tennessee, Xavier University, Texas A&M University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date:

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Margaret Ruxton,

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
[LibNote]Between 1817 & 1821[/LibNote] [LibNote] 59 [LibNote] [LibNote] 5) [/LibNote] I agree with you that Riley is beyond the truth - but [deletion]I am glad[/deletion], liar or not that is his affair - I am glad he entertains my uncle - and I hope my uncle's snuffers have come to hand & that he is not angry with me for sending him such a vulgar souvenir - Thank dear little Tom for remembering [underline]Malia[/underline] and [underline]Jenny[/underline] - I admire little John's performance of his manifold duties - I never thought he was little John Nobody - he has now proved himself to be little John Somebody, and if he stands the trial of the new coat & tucker to shirt I do not despair of his walking with unblenched virtue through the mud= -ordeal & vice=ordeal of Navan - worse than any fire or water ordeal or any burning plough shares on which Q Emma or others ever walked ! - There is said to be some marvellous moral or immoral fracas in the Devonshire family; but I have not got the story by the right end yet - So, for fear of spoiling a choice bit of scandal in the telling, shall wait till I [underline]am perfect[/underline] in it - and mean time perhaps you may have it fresh & fresh from my mother - or the newspapers - I am glad Sophy goes to dear M[superscript]rs.[/superscript] P. Ruxton
Me having deprived her of that visit long sat heavy as the night mare on my conscience - - To Miss Garnett alias M[superscript]rs.[/superscript] Ratcliffe's other sins must be a [unclear]alloted[/unclear] that of preventing Sophy from must be added that of preventing Sophy from seeing the archdeacons pictures! - Well! when a person is down in the moral world sins enough are always ready to be heaped upon Their heads - I think [deletion]mit[/deletion] Ellen G- is just a heroine & [insertion]hers just[/insertion] [underline]a[/underline] story fit for the author of North- -anger Abbey - and no letter in any novel was ever better in its own way than [underline]hers[/underline] - Sneyd and his gout go on finely - Poor M[superscript]rs.[/superscript] Sneyd E: as headachy as usual - [deletion]William and[/deletion] Lovell returns to us on Wed= nesday - Honora is well - And I have nothing more to say - except that I am sorry M[superscript]r.[/superscript] R. Dillon & the Bishop are going to war - - But in fact there must be war about nonsense in this world. - I wonder that anxiety about M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. O OBeirne does not in the bishop's mind swallow up all other feelings - I am most sincerely sorry for her - Send her Miss Bailly's letter to amuse her. As soon as I have my Jewess's letter [insertion]again[/insertion] you shall have it - Adieu dearest Aunt - What pleasure I shall have in being with you again - when I have [underline]earned [/underline]that pleasure - - The greatest I think I have now left upon earth - Maria Edgeworth
